name: Barakah Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: First commercial nuclear power station in the Arab world, providing up to 25% of UAE electricity.
notes: Unit 4 entered commercial service in September 2024.

name: Jebel Ali Power and Desalination Complex
strategicNote: World's largest single-site natural gas power generation facility and critical desalination hub for Dubai.

name: Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum Solar Park
strategicNote: World's largest single-site solar park, core of Dubai's Clean Energy Strategy 2050.
notes: Capacity as of 2024; planned to reach 5000 MW by 2030.

name: Al Taweelah Power Plant
strategicNote: Major power and water production site serving Abu Dhabi.

name: Hassyan Thermal Plant
strategicNote: Originally designed as clean coal, converted to natural gas to align with UAE's net zero goals.
notes: Full capacity reached in 2023.

name: Al Dhafra Solar PV
owner: TAQA, Masdar, EDF Renewables, Jinko Power
strategicNote: One of the world's largest single-site solar PV plants.

name: Fujairah F2 IWPP
strategicNote: Major independent water and power project on the east coast.

name: Noor Abu Dhabi
strategicNote: Pioneering large-scale solar project in Abu Dhabi.

name: Shuweihat S3 Power Plant
strategicNote: Part of the Shuweihat complex, a key power generation site in Abu Dhabi's western region.

name: Hamriyah Power Plant
operator: Sharjah Electricity, Water and Gas Authority
strategicNote: Most efficient gas power plant in the UAE, critical for Sharjah's power supply.

name: Atucha I Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: First nuclear power plant in Latin America. Currently undergoing life extension works.
notes: Net capacity is 319 MW. Life extension expected to last until 2026/27.

name: Atucha II Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Largest nuclear reactor in Argentina, significantly boosting base-load capacity.
notes: Net capacity is 692 MW.

name: Embalse Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Critical facility providing baseload power and producing Cobalt-60 for medical use.
notes: Completed life extension in 2019. Net capacity 608 MW.

name: Yacyretá Hydroelectric Dam
strategicNote: Argentina's largest power producer, providing roughly 15% of national demand.
notes: Binational facility on the Paraná River.

name: Salto Grande Hydroelectric Plant
operator: Comisión Técnica Mixta de Salto Grande
strategicNote: Major binational hydro facility on the Uruguay River.
notes: Argentina share is 945 MW.

name: Piedra del Águila Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Key peaking plant in the Comahue region.

name: El Chocón Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Major hydroelectric facility on the Limay River.

name: Cauchari Solar Park
strategicNote: One of the highest solar parks in the world, developed with Chinese financing and technology.
notes: Comprises Cauchari I, II, and III (100 MW each).

name: Termoeléctrica Manuel Belgrano
strategicNote: Major combined cycle gas plant serving the Greater Buenos Aires area.

name: Central Térmica José de San Martín (Timbúes)
operator: Termoeléctrica José de San Martín S.A.
strategicNote: Critical gas-fired plant serving the industrial belt of Rosario.

name: Rooppur Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Bangladesh's first nuclear power plant, built with Russian assistance; a cornerstone of the country's energy diversification strategy.
notes: Consists of two VVER-1200 reactors.

name: Ashuganj Power Station
strategicNote: One of the largest gas-fired hubs in Bangladesh, strategically located near the Titas gas field.
notes: Total capacity of various units at the Ashuganj hub.

name: Payra Thermal Power Plant
strategicNote: Bangladesh's first ultra-supercritical coal plant, vital for southern grid stability.
notes: Joint venture between NWPGCL and China National Machinery Import and Export Corporation.

name: Rampal Power Station (Maitree Super Thermal)
strategicNote: A joint venture between Bangladesh and India, controversial due to its proximity to the Sundarbans mangrove forest.

name: Matarbari Power Station
strategicNote: Key project in the Maheshkhali energy hub, supported by Japanese financing (JICA); includes a deep-sea port for coal imports.

name: Ghorasal Power Plant
strategicNote: Major gas-fired facility serving the Dhaka industrial area; recently upgraded for higher efficiency.

name: Sirajganj Power Station
strategicNote: Important gas-fired hub in western Bangladesh.
notes: Includes several combined cycle units.

name: Barapukuria Power Station
strategicNote: The only coal-fired power plant in Bangladesh using domestic coal from the adjacent Barapukuria mine.

name: Karnafuli Hydroelectric Power Station
strategicNote: Bangladesh's only major hydroelectric facility; critical for the Chittagong Hill Tracts region.

name: Rupsha Power Station
strategicNote: Large LNG-based power plant under construction to meet growing demand in southwestern Bangladesh.

name: Itaipu Hydroelectric Dam
strategicNote: World's second largest hydroelectric plant by capacity and often first by energy production. Critical for both Brazil and Paraguay's grids.
notes: Binational facility on the Paraná River.

name: Belo Monte Hydroelectric Complex
owner: Norte Energia (Consortium of Eletrobras, PETROS, FUNCEF, etc.)
strategicNote: Largest entirely Brazilian-owned hydroelectric plant. Highly controversial due to environmental and indigenous impact on the Xingu River.
notes: Full capacity reached in 2019.

name: Tucuruí Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Major power source for the northern region of Brazil and the aluminum industry.

name: Angra 1 Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Brazil's first nuclear power plant, vital for the Rio de Janeiro regional grid.
notes: Net capacity 609 MW.

name: Angra 2 Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Significant baseload power source for southeastern Brazil.
notes: Net capacity 1275 MW.

name: Jirau Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Major run-of-the-river plant on the Madeira River, part of the Madeira River Hydroelectric Complex.

name: Santo Antônio Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Critical facility on the Madeira River utilizing bulb turbines for lower head.

name: Porto de Sergipe I
strategicNote: Largest natural gas-fired power plant in Latin America, integrated with an FSRU.

name: Pirapora Solar Complex
strategicNote: One of Brazil's largest solar complexes, representing the shift towards large-scale PV.

name: Ilha Solteira Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Major hydroelectric plant on the Paraná River, part of the Urubupungá complex.

name: Three Gorges Dam
strategicNote: World's largest power station by installed capacity; critical for flood control on the Yangtze River and China's renewable energy goals.
notes: Full capacity reached in 2012.

name: Fuqing Nuclear Power Plant
operator: CNNC Fujian Fuqing Nuclear Power Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: First site to deploy the Hualong One (HPR1000) indigenous Gen-III reactor design.

name: Yangjiang Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: One of the largest nuclear power complexes in China, featuring six 1,000 MW units.

name: Tianwan Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: Largest nuclear power cooperation project between China and Russia; expanding to exceed 8,000 MW.

name: Hongyanhe Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Liaoning Hongyanhe Nuclear Power Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: The first nuclear power plant in Northeast China and the largest in the region.

name: Ningde Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Fujian Ningde Nuclear Power Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: A major component of Fujian's energy infrastructure, supporting the regional industrial hub.

name: Qinshan Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: China's first domestically designed and built nuclear power plant; the site has multiple phases and reactor types.
notes: Includes Qinshan Phases I, II, and III.

name: Daya Bay Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Daya Bay Nuclear Power Operations and Management Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: Historically significant as China's first major commercial nuclear project, largely serving Hong Kong.

name: Ling Ao Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Daya Bay Nuclear Power Operations and Management Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: Adjacent to Daya Bay, this plant is a key power supplier to the Pearl River Delta.

name: Sanmen Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: First site to deploy the AP1000 Gen-III reactor technology designed by Westinghouse.
notes: Additional units under construction.

name: Haiyang Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Another key site for AP1000 technology; notable for its large-scale district heating project using waste heat.
notes: Additional units under construction.

name: Taishan Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Taishan Nuclear Power Joint Venture Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: World's first site to put the European Pressurized Reactor (EPR) into commercial operation.

name: Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Guangxi Fangchenggang Nuclear Power Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: Strategic nuclear hub in Southwest China, near the border with Vietnam; deploying Hualong One units.

name: Changjiang Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: The first nuclear power plant on Hainan Island, critical for the island's clean energy transition.

name: Shidaowan Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Huaneng Shandong Shidao Bay Nuclear Power Co., Ltd.
strategicNote: World's first commercial high-temperature gas-cooled reactor (HTGR), representing Gen-IV nuclear technology.
notes: HTR-PM unit.

name: Neurath Power Station
strategicNote: Germany's largest power station, primarily lignite-fired; a central point in the debate over coal phase-out.
notes: Includes several units, including the 1,100 MW 'BoA' units.

name: Niederaussem Power Station
strategicNote: Major lignite-fired station in the Rhineland, significant for base-load power in industrial Western Germany.

name: Jänschwalde Power Station
strategicNote: Largest power station in Eastern Germany, critical for the regional economy and grid stability.

name: Boxberg Power Station
strategicNote: Significant lignite facility in Lusatia, key to the coal phase-out timeline in the East.

name: Schwarze Pumpe Power Station
strategicNote: Highly efficient lignite plant, often cited in discussions about modernizing coal power.

name: Isar Nuclear Power Plant (Unit 2)
strategicNote: One of the last three nuclear plants to be shut down in April 2023, marking the end of nuclear power in Germany.
notes: Shutdown as part of the Atomausstieg policy.

name: Emsland Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: One of the final three nuclear reactors shut down in 2023.

name: Neckarwestheim Nuclear Power Plant (Unit 2)
strategicNote: The third of the final nuclear plants closed in April 2023.

name: Irsching Power Station
strategicNote: A critical gas-fired backup facility, essential for grid stability as renewable share grows.
notes: Includes highly efficient CCGT units 4 and 5.

name: Goldisthal Pumped Storage Station
strategicNote: Germany's largest pumped-storage plant, acting as a massive battery for the national grid.

name: Markersbach Pumped Storage Station
strategicNote: Second largest pumped-storage facility, critical for frequency regulation.

name: Brokdorf Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Major nuclear plant shut down in 2021; historically a site of significant anti-nuclear protests.

name: Grohnde Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Shut down in 2021, it was one of Germany's most productive nuclear plants.

name: Gundremmingen Nuclear Power Plant (Unit C)
strategicNote: Last unit of the Gundremmingen site to be shut down in 2021.

name: Datteln 4 Power Station
strategicNote: Germany's newest and most controversial hard coal plant, commissioned in 2020.

name: El Dabaa Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Egypt's first nuclear power plant, a flagship project to diversify energy sources and establish nuclear technology in the country.
notes: Consists of four VVER-1200 reactors.

name: Aswan High Dam
strategicNote: Historically the most significant infrastructure project in modern Egypt, providing electricity and controlling Nile floods.

name: Benban Solar Park
strategicNote: One of the world's largest solar installations, central to Egypt's goal of generating 42% of its energy from renewables by 2035.
notes: Composed of 41 individual solar plants.

name: New Capital Power Plant
strategicNote: One of the three Siemens megaprojects that ended Egypt's electricity deficit.
notes: Largest gas-fired plant in Egypt.

name: Beni Suef Power Plant
strategicNote: Critical combined-cycle gas plant serving Middle Egypt, part of the Siemens megaproject.

name: Burullus Power Plant
strategicNote: Major coastal power plant serving the Delta region, part of the Siemens megaproject.

name: Gulf of El Zayt Wind Farm
strategicNote: Major wind power facility on the Red Sea coast, taking advantage of high wind speeds in the area.

name: Ain Sokhna Power Plant
strategicNote: Major supercritical thermal plant serving the Suez industrial zone.
notes: Dual-fuel capability (Gas/Oil).

name: West Damietta Power Plant
strategicNote: Key gas plant in the northern Delta region, supporting the industrial port of Damietta.

name: Ras Ghareb Wind Farm
strategicNote: First independent power producer (IPP) wind project in Egypt.

name: Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam (GERD)
strategicNote: Largest hydroelectric plant in Africa and a major source of regional tension with downstream countries Egypt and Sudan over Nile water rights.
notes: Current installed capacity; planned final capacity is 6,450 MW.

name: Gilgel Gibe III Dam
strategicNote: Critical for Ethiopia's electricity exports to neighboring Kenya.

name: Tana Beles Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Major inter-basin transfer project utilizing water from Lake Tana for power and irrigation.

name: Tekeze Dam
strategicNote: Double-curvature arch dam on the Tekeze River, critical for the northern region's power supply.

name: Adama Wind Farm (I & II)
strategicNote: First major wind farm in Ethiopia, demonstrating the potential for non-hydro renewables.
notes: Adama I (51 MW) and Adama II (153 MW).

name: Gilgel Gibe II Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Unique facility using the head between Gibe I and Gibe II reservoirs through a 26km tunnel.

name: Ashegoda Wind Farm
strategicNote: Key wind facility in the Tigray region.

name: Reppie Waste-to-Energy Plant
strategicNote: Africa's first major waste-to-energy facility, addressing waste management and power needs in Addis Ababa.

name: Fincha Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: One of Ethiopia's older but still critical hydroelectric facilities on the Fincha River.

name: Aluto Langano Geothermal Plant
strategicNote: Ethiopia's pilot geothermal facility, proving the immense Rift Valley geothermal potential.
notes: Expansion projects are underway to significantly increase capacity.

name: Gravelines Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: One of the largest nuclear power plants in Europe, critical for Northern France and exports to the UK and Benelux.
notes: Six units of 910 MW each.

name: Paluel Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: High-capacity plant located on the English Channel, key for Normandy and Paris regions.
notes: Four units of 1330 MW each.

name: Cattenom Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Located near the borders of Luxembourg and Germany, highly significant for regional power balance and cross-border relations.
notes: Four units of 1300 MW each.

name: Flamanville Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Site of the first French EPR reactor (Unit 3), symbolizing the future of French nuclear technology despite delays.
notes: Includes two 1300 MW units and one 1600 MW EPR unit (commissioning 2024).

name: Chinon Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: A major hub in central France, essential for the Loire valley and Western France.
notes: Four units of 900 MW class.

name: Cruas Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Key facility in the Rhone valley, providing power to the industrial South-East.
notes: Four units of 900 MW class.

name: Tricastin Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Part of a major nuclear industrial complex including uranium enrichment facilities.
notes: Four units of 900 MW class.

name: Blayais Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Vital for the power supply of the Bordeaux region and South-West France.
notes: Four units of 900 MW class.

name: Bugey Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Critical for the Lyon metropolitan area and industrial centers in the Rhone-Alpes.
notes: Four units of 900 MW class operating.

name: Dampierre Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Centrally located plant feeding the national grid and supporting the Paris region.
notes: Four units of 900 MW class.

name: Chooz Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Located in a protrusion into Belgium, essential for northern grid stability and exports.
notes: Two units of 1500 MW each.

name: Civaux Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Features some of the most modern reactors in the French fleet.
notes: Two units of 1450 MW each.

name: Saint-Alban Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Strategically located in the Rhone valley near Lyon.
notes: Two units of 1300 MW class.

name: Penly Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Important coastal plant in Normandy, designated as a site for future EPR2 reactors.
notes: Two units of 1300 MW class.

name: Belleville Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Critical for central France and the Loire valley power supply.
notes: Two units of 1300 MW class.

name: Hinkley Point C Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: The first new nuclear plant in the UK in over 20 years, critical for meeting net-zero targets and future energy security.
notes: Consists of two EPR reactors.

name: Pembroke Power Station
strategicNote: The UK's largest and most efficient CCGT power station, vital for flexible grid support.

name: Staythorpe C Power Station
strategicNote: A major contributor to the UK's natural gas generation fleet.

name: Dinorwig Power Station
strategicNote: The UK's largest pumped-storage facility, essential for rapid response to grid frequency fluctuations.
notes: Built inside a mountain in Snowdonia.

name: Didcot B Power Station
strategicNote: Key gas-fired station serving the South of England.

name: Connah's Quay Power Station
strategicNote: Significant CCGT plant in North Wales.

name: West Burton B Power Station
strategicNote: Modern gas plant located on the site of a former coal station.

name: South Humber Bank Power Station
strategicNote: A major industrial power hub on the Humber estuary.

name: Heysham 2 Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: The UK's most advanced Advanced Gas-cooled Reactor (AGR) station.

name: Saltend Power Station
strategicNote: Key gas plant in the Humber region, part of the Zero Carbon Humber initiative.

name: Sizewell B Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: The UK's only Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) currently in operation.

name: Peterhead Power Station
strategicNote: Critical plant for grid stability in Scotland; site of a major CCS project proposal.

name: Torness Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: Key nuclear asset for Scotland's power supply.

name: Hartlepool Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: Major employer and power source in North-East England.

name: Ratcliffe-on-Soar Power Station
strategicNote: The UK's last operating coal-fired power station, shut down in September 2024, marking the end of the UK's coal era.

name: Paiton Power Station
strategicNote: One of the largest coal-fired power complexes in Southeast Asia, critical for the Java-Bali grid.
notes: Includes multiple units owned by different entities including Mitsui and Nebras Power.

name: Tanjung Jati B Power Station
strategicNote: A major baseload provider for the Central Java region.
notes: Units 5 & 6 (Java 4) recently added significant capacity.

name: Suralaya Power Station
strategicNote: The largest power plant in Indonesia, vital for supplying electricity to the Jakarta metropolitan area.
notes: Located at the western tip of Java.

name: Cirata Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: The largest hydroelectric plant in Southeast Asia, also hosts a major floating solar farm.

name: Java 7 (Banten) Power Station
operator: PT Shenhua Guohua Pembangkitan Jawa Bali
strategicNote: Uses ultra-supercritical technology, representing modern large-scale coal infrastructure in Indonesia.

name: Batang Power Station (Central Java Power Plant)
owner: Electric Power Development (J-Power) / Itochu / Adaro Power
strategicNote: A major Independent Power Producer (IPP) project significantly boosting Central Java's capacity.

name: Wayang Windu Geothermal Power Station
strategicNote: One of the most efficient geothermal plants in the world, showcasing Indonesia's massive geothermal potential.

name: Sarulla Geothermal Power Station
owner: Medco Power / Itochu / Kyushu Electric / Inpex
strategicNote: The world's largest single-contract geothermal power project.

name: Muara Karang Power Station
strategicNote: Critical gas-fired plant supplying the heart of Jakarta.
notes: Includes modern combined-cycle units.

name: Indonesia Morowali Industrial Park (IMIP) Captive Power
strategicNote: Massive captive coal power complex driving the world's largest nickel processing hub.
notes: Strategically critical for the global EV battery supply chain.

name: Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: India's largest nuclear power plant, built in collaboration with Russia; a major component of Indo-Russian strategic cooperation.
notes: Four additional units are currently under construction.

name: Vindhyachal Thermal Power Station
strategicNote: Largest thermal power plant in India; a critical hub for the country's coal-based electricity generation.
notes: Comprises multiple units commissioned in stages from 1987 to 2015.

name: Mundra Thermal Power Station
strategicNote: A major private sector power plant and a key part of the Mundra Special Economic Zone; uses supercritical technology.

name: Bhadla Solar Park
owner: Multiple (Rajasthan Solar Park Development Company Limited and others)
strategicNote: One of the world's largest solar parks, located in the Thar Desert; a flagship project for India's renewable energy ambitions.
notes: Developed in four phases.

name: Pavagada Solar Park
operator: Karnataka Solar Power Development Corporation Ltd
strategicNote: Massive solar park in Karnataka, part of the 'Shakti Sthala' initiative to boost solar capacity.

name: Tarapur Atomic Power Station
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: India's first commercial nuclear power station; houses both Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and Pressurized Heavy Water Reactors (PHWRs).

name: Rajasthan Atomic Power Station
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: Major nuclear facility in Northern India; home to multiple PHWR units.
notes: Unit 1 is currently under extended shutdown.

name: Kakrapar Atomic Power Station
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: Notable for commissioning Unit 3 in 2023, the first of India's indigenous 700 MWe PHWR series.

name: Kaiga Atomic Power Station
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: Strategic nuclear facility in Southern India; Unit 1 holds a world record for continuous operation.

name: Koyna Hydroelectric Project
strategicNote: India's largest completed hydroelectric power plant; critical for peaking power and grid stability in Maharashtra.

name: Tehri Dam
strategicNote: Highest dam in India and one of the highest in the world; a multi-purpose rock and earth-fill embankment dam.
notes: Capacity listed is for the HPP (Hydro Power Plant) stage.

name: Madras Atomic Power Station
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: India's first fully indigenous nuclear power station; also the site for the Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor (PFBR).

name: Narora Atomic Power Station
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: Nuclear power plant located in Uttar Pradesh, critical for the Northern power grid.

name: Jaisalmer Wind Park
strategicNote: One of the largest onshore wind farms in India, located in Rajasthan; a key part of India's wind energy capacity.

name: Kudankulam Units 3-6 (Construction)
operator: Nuclear Power Corporation of India Limited (NPCIL)
strategicNote: Expansion of the Kudankulam site, which will make it one of the largest nuclear complexes in the world.
notes: Estimated commissioning for first units in 2024-2025.

name: Bushehr Nuclear Power Plant (Unit 1)
operator: Nuclear Power Production and Development Company of Iran
strategicNote: Iran's first and only operational nuclear power plant, a central point of international geopolitical interest.
notes: Built with Russian assistance. Units 2 and 3 are under construction.

name: Damavand (Pakdasht) Combined Cycle Power Plant
strategicNote: The largest thermal power plant in Iran and one of the largest in the Middle East, critical for Tehran's stability.

name: Shahid Salimi (Neka) Power Plant
strategicNote: A major facility in northern Iran, capable of using both gas and fuel oil.

name: Karun-3 Hydroelectric Power Plant
operator: Iran Water and Power Resources Development Co.
strategicNote: The largest hydroelectric plant in Iran, providing critical peak power and grid stability.

name: Masjed Soleyman Hydroelectric Power Plant
operator: Iran Water and Power Resources Development Co.
strategicNote: A key component of the Karun river cascade system.

name: Shahid Rajaee Power Plant
strategicNote: Major thermal plant serving the Qazvin and Tehran industrial corridors.
notes: Includes both steam and gas units.

name: Ramin Power Plant
strategicNote: Largest thermal power plant in southwestern Iran, critical for the oil-rich Khuzestan province.

name: Shahid Montazeri Power Plant
strategicNote: Major power source for the central industrial city of Isfahan.

name: Gotvand Hydroelectric Power Plant
operator: Iran Water and Power Resources Development Co.
strategicNote: High-capacity hydro plant; has faced environmental controversies due to salt domes in its reservoir.

name: Siah Bishe Pumped Storage Power Plant
operator: Iran Water and Power Resources Development Co.
strategicNote: Iran's first pumped-storage plant, essential for grid frequency control and peak shaving near Tehran.

name: Orot Rabin Power Station
strategicNote: Israel's largest power station, currently undergoing conversion from coal to natural gas for some units.
notes: Strategically vital for Israel's energy independence and baseload supply.

name: Rutenberg Power Station
strategicNote: The second largest power plant in Israel, located near the southern border.
notes: Key facility for the southern grid.

name: Eshkol Power Station
strategicNote: Recently privatized; a major natural gas facility near Ashdod port.
notes: Includes modern combined-cycle units.

name: Hagit Power Station
strategicNote: Major natural gas combined cycle plant in northern Israel.

name: Gezer Power Station
strategicNote: Large natural gas facility serving the central district.

name: Dalia (Tzafit) Power Station
strategicNote: The largest private power plant in Israel.

name: Dorad Power Station
strategicNote: Major private natural gas plant located at the EAPC site.

name: Ashalim Solar Thermal Power Station
strategicNote: Flagship renewable project in the Negev desert, including one of the world's tallest solar towers.
notes: Combines CSP and PV technologies.

name: Kashiwazaki-Kariwa Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: The largest nuclear power station in the world by net capacity; its restart is a key part of Japan's energy strategy post-Fukushima.
notes: Currently suspended pending safety upgrades and regulatory approval.

name: Futtsu Power Station
strategicNote: A massive LNG-fired plant, critical for supplying the Tokyo metropolitan area.

name: Ohi Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: One of Japan's largest operating nuclear plants, vital for the Kansai region's industrial base.
notes: Units 3 and 4 are currently operating.

name: Hekinan Power Station
strategicNote: Japan's largest coal-fired power station; currently testing large-scale ammonia co-firing to reduce emissions.

name: Sodegaura Power Station
strategicNote: Major LNG-fired station on Tokyo Bay.

name: Kawasaki Power Station
strategicNote: Modern CCGT plant serving the industrial heart of the Keihin region.

name: Takahama Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: A major nuclear hub for Western Japan; all four units have received approval to operate beyond 40 years.

name: Chiba Power Station
strategicNote: Significant gas-fired capacity near Tokyo.

name: Kannagawa Hydropower Plant
strategicNote: The world's largest pumped-storage power station when complete, providing massive peak-shaving capacity.

name: Higashi-Niigata Power Station
strategicNote: Major gas hub on the Sea of Japan coast.

name: Matsuura Power Station
strategicNote: Large coal facility in Kyushu, important for base-load in the south.

name: Genkai Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Key nuclear facility in Kyushu; Units 3 and 4 were among the first to restart after Fukushima.

name: Hitachinaka Power Station
strategicNote: Major coal plant serving the Kanto region.

name: Okutataragi Pumped Storage Station
strategicNote: Japan's second largest pumped-storage facility, critical for the Kansai grid.

name: Sendai Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: The first nuclear plant to restart under Japan's new post-Fukushima safety standards in 2015.

name: Ekibastuz GRES-1
strategicNote: The largest power station in Kazakhstan, vital for the country's heavy industry and export capacity.
notes: One of the largest coal-fired plants in the world.

name: Ekibastuz GRES-2
strategicNote: Features the world's tallest chimney (419.7 m). Strategically important for northern Kazakhstan's grid.
notes: Expansion plans to increase capacity to 4000 MW are ongoing.

name: Aksu Power Station
strategicNote: Major power source for the Aksu Ferroalloy Plant, one of the largest in the world.

name: Zhambyl GRES
strategicNote: The main power source for southern Kazakhstan, capable of using gas or fuel oil.

name: Shulbinsk Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: The largest hydroelectric plant in Kazakhstan, located on the Irtysh River.

name: Bukhtarma Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: Major hydro plant providing peak power and supporting mining operations in East Kazakhstan.

name: Ulken (Balkhash) Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Proposed first commercial nuclear power plant in Kazakhstan, approved by referendum in 2024.
notes: Strategically critical for reducing coal dependency and meeting future energy deficits.

name: MAEK-Kazatomprom (Mangistau)
strategicNote: Critical energy and desalination hub for the Mangistau region; site of the decommissioned BN-350 breeder reactor.
notes: Now primarily gas-fired.

name: Olkaria I Geothermal Power Station
strategicNote: Part of the massive Olkaria geothermal complex, which provides nearly half of Kenya's electricity.
notes: Includes the recently commissioned Olkaria I Unit 6.

name: Olkaria V Geothermal Power Station

name: Olkaria IV Geothermal Power Station

strategicNote: The largest wind farm in Africa, providing a significant portion of Kenya's renewable energy.
notes: Features 365 turbines.

name: Gitaru Hydroelectric Power Station
strategicNote: The largest hydroelectric plant in Kenya, part of the Seven Forks Scheme.

name: Kiambere Hydroelectric Power Station
strategicNote: A key component of the Tana River hydro cascade.

name: Turkwel Hydroelectric Power Station

name: Kipeto Wind Farm

name: Taean Power Station

name: Kori & Saeul Nuclear Power Plant

name: Hanbit Nuclear Power Plant

name: Yeongheung Power Station

name: Wolsong Nuclear Power Plant

name: Hadong Power Station
strategicNote: Major coal-fired hub in the south, supporting the Gyeongsang region.

name: Boryeong Power Station

name: Incheon LNG Power Plant

name: Samcheok Green Power Plant

name: Yangyang Pumped Storage Power Plant

name: Chungju Hydroelectric Plant

name: Sihwa Lake Tidal Power Station

name: Shin Hanul Nuclear Power Plant

notes: Units 1 and 2 (1400 MW each). Unit 1 commissioned in 2022, Unit 2 in 2024.

name: Petacalco (Plutarco Elías Calles) Power Plant

name: Manuel Moreno Torres (Chicoasén) Dam

name: Tuxpan (Adolfo López Mateos) Power Plant

name: Laguna Verde Nuclear Power Plant

notes: Two Boiling Water Reactors (BWR).

name: Empalme Combined Cycle Power Plant
strategicNote: Major modern natural gas facility supporting the industrial northwest.

name: Tula (Francisco Pérez Ríos) Power Plant

name: Carbon II Power Plant

name: Manzanillo I (Manuel Álvarez Moreno) Power Plant

name: Carbon I (José López Portillo) Power Plant

name: Altamira V Power Plant

name: Infiernillo (Adolfo López Mateos) Dam
strategicNote: Strategically important hydroelectric facility on the Balsas River.

name: Malpaso (Nezahualcóyotl) Dam

name: Aguamilpa (Solidaridad) Dam

name: Sultan Azlan Shah (Manjung) Power Station

name: Tanjung Bin Power Station

name: Edra Melaka Power Plant (EMPP)

name: Lumut Segari Power Plant
strategicNote: One of the largest gas-fired IPPs in Malaysia, supporting the Perak and northern regions.

name: Pengerang Cogeneration Plant
strategicNote: Supplies electricity and steam to the Pengerang Integrated Complex (PIC), a massive oil and gas hub in Johor.

name: Murum Hydroelectric Dam
strategicNote: Second major hydro project in Sarawak, following Bakun, critical for industrial expansion in East Malaysia.

name: Pergau Dam

name: Tuanku Jaafar Power Station
strategicNote: Large gas-fired plant near Port Dickson; essential for the central Peninsular grid.

name: Baleh Hydroelectric Project
strategicNote: Massive hydro project under construction in Sarawak; will be a major contributor to the ASEAN Power Grid.

name: Egbin Thermal Power Station

name: Alaoji Power Station

name: Ughelli (Delta) Power Plant
strategicNote: One of Nigeria's oldest and largest gas-fired power plants.

name: Kainji Dam

name: Zungeru Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: Newly commissioned large-scale hydro facility, a major boost to national capacity.

name: Olorunsogo II Power Station
strategicNote: Key NIPP combined cycle plant in Ogun State.

name: Afam VI Power Plant

name: Shiroro Dam

name: Jebba Hydroelectric Power Station

name: Geregu I & II Power Stations

name: Azura-Edo IPP
strategicNote: Nigeria's first privately financed independent power plant (IPP).

name: Geregu Nuclear Power Plant

notes: Planned for 2 x 1200 MW VVER reactors.

name: Itu Nuclear Power Plant

name: Eems Power Plant (Eemscentrale)

name: Eemshaven Power Station
strategicNote: One of the most modern and largest coal-fired plants in Europe, facing phase-out by 2029.

name: Hollandse Kust Zuid Offshore Wind Farm

name: Magnum Power Station

name: Claus Power Plant

name: Maasvlakte Power Plant (MPP3)

name: Hollandse Kust Noord Offshore Wind Farm

name: Onyx Centrale Rotterdam
strategicNote: Coal-fired plant in Rotterdam, subject to national coal phase-out laws.

name: Gemini Wind Farm

strategicNote: One of the world's largest offshore wind farms at the time of commissioning, located far offshore.

name: Borssele Nuclear Power Station

name: Windplan Groen

name: Karachi Nuclear Power Complex (KANUPP-2 & 3)

name: Chashma Nuclear Power Plant (C-1 to C-4)

notes: Capacity represents combined output of units C-1, C-2, C-3, and C-4.

name: Tarbela Dam Hydroelectric Plant

name: Ghazi-Barotha Hydropower Project
strategicNote: A major run-of-the-river project on the Indus River, providing baseload power without a large reservoir.

name: Mangla Dam Hydroelectric Plant
strategicNote: Second largest dam in Pakistan; critical for water storage under the Indus Waters Treaty and power generation.
notes: Ongoing refurbishment is expected to increase capacity to 1500 MW.

name: Neelum-Jhelum Hydropower Plant

name: Sahiwal Coal Power Plant

name: Port Qasim Coal Power Plant
strategicNote: Major CPEC coal plant located at Karachi's port, utilizing imported coal for efficient southern grid supply.

name: China Power Hub Generation Company (Hubco)
strategicNote: Significant CPEC investment in Balochistan, supplying power to the national grid via the Hub-Karachi link.

name: Guddu Thermal Power Station
strategicNote: One of the oldest and largest thermal power stations in Pakistan, utilizing local gas reserves.

name: Bin Qasim Power Station (BQPS)
strategicNote: The primary power source for Karachi; recently upgraded with high-efficiency BQPS-III combined cycle units.

name: Haveli Bahadur Shah Power Plant
strategicNote: A high-efficiency RLNG (Re-gasified Liquefied Natural Gas) plant, part of the government's drive to end power shortages.

name: Thar Block I Coal Power Plant
operator: Thar Coal Block-I Power Generation Co.
strategicNote: Utilizes indigenous lignite coal from the Thar desert, a major milestone in Pakistan's energy independence strategy.

name: Dasu Hydropower Project
strategicNote: A massive run-of-river project on the Indus River; critical for the next decade's renewable energy targets.

name: Bataan Nuclear Power Plant

name: Santa Rita & San Lorenzo Gas Power Plants

name: Ilijan Power Plant

name: Sual Power Station
strategicNote: The largest coal-fired power plant in the Philippines, providing a significant portion of Luzon's baseload power.

name: Dinginin Power Station

name: Pagbilao Power Station

name: Masinloc Power Plant

name: Kalayaan Pumped Storage Power Plant

name: Tiwi Geothermal Power Plant

name: Burgos Wind Farm

name: Bełchatów Power Station

name: Turów Power Station

name: Jaworzno III Power Station

name: PGE Gryfino Dolna Odra CCGT

name: Płock CCGT

name: Novovoronezh Nuclear Power Plant

name: Riyadh Power Plant 9 (PP9)

name: Ghazlan Power Plant

name: Ratchaburi Power Plant

name: South Bangkok Power Plant

name: Srinagarind Dam

name: Atatürk Dam

name: Karakaya Dam

name: Keban Dam

name: Enka Gebze Power Plant

name: Enka İzmir Power Plant

name: Hamitabat Power Plant

name: Cenal Power Plant

name: Soma B Power Plant

name: Sugözü Power Plant

name: Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant

name: Rivne Nuclear Power Plant

name: South Ukraine Nuclear Power Plant

name: Khmelnytskyi Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Target for future expansion with Western reactor technology to reduce dependence on Russian designs.

name: Dnipro Hydroelectric Station

name: Dniester Hydroelectric Station
strategicNote: Vital for grid maneuvering and water regulation in Southwest Ukraine and Moldova.

name: Burshtyn Power Plant

notes: Target of numerous missile strikes.

name: Trypilska Power Plant
strategicNote: Completely destroyed by Russian strikes in April 2024; was the largest power supplier for the Kyiv region.

name: Zmiivska Power Plant
strategicNote: One of the largest thermal power plants in the Kharkiv region; completely destroyed in March 2024.

name: Dnistrovska Pumped Storage Power Station
strategicNote: One of the largest pumped-storage plants in Europe, essential for maintaining grid frequency.

name: Kryvorizka Power Plant
strategicNote: Major thermal plant in Central Ukraine, damaged by shelling due to its proximity to the front lines.

name: Ladyzhynska Power Plant
strategicNote: Significant thermal power plant in Vinnytsia region, frequently targeted by strikes.

name: Kaniv Hydroelectric Station
strategicNote: Part of the Dnieper cascade, important for peak load coverage and water management.

name: Kyiv Hydroelectric Station
strategicNote: First stage of the Dnieper cascade, vital for Kyiv's energy and water safety.

name: Tashlyk Pumped Storage Power Station
strategicNote: Integral part of the South Ukraine Energy Complex, assisting in nuclear load leveling.

name: Grand Coulee Dam
strategicNote: Largest power station in the United States; critical infrastructure for the Pacific Northwest power grid and irrigation.

name: Vogtle Electric Generating Plant
owner: Georgia Power (Southern Company), Oglethorpe Power, MEAG Power, Dalton Utilities
strategicNote: Largest nuclear plant in the U.S. following the 2024 completion of Units 3 and 4, the first new nuclear units in the U.S. in over 30 years.
notes: Units 3 and 4 are AP1000 reactors.

name: Palo Verde Generating Station
owner: Arizona Public Service, Salt River Project, Southern California Edison, and others
strategicNote: Largest nuclear plant in the U.S. by net generation; unique for being the only large nuclear plant not located near a large body of water (uses treated sewage effluent for cooling).

name: Browns Ferry Nuclear Plant
strategicNote: First nuclear plant in the world to generate 1,000 MW from a single unit; critical base-load power for the TVA service area.

name: Robert W. Scherer Power Plant

strategicNote: One of the largest coal-fired power plants in the United States; historically a major point source of CO2 emissions.
notes: Multiple units have been retired or are planned for retirement in the coming years.

name: Peach Bottom Atomic Power Station
strategicNote: Strategically located near the Susquehanna River; has undergone significant power uprates to increase capacity.

name: South Texas Project Electric Generating Station
owner: NRG Energy, CPS Energy, Austin Energy
strategicNote: Critical power source for the Texas (ERCOT) grid; notable for its large cooling reservoir.

name: Oconee Nuclear Station
strategicNote: Major nuclear facility in the Carolinas; one of the first nuclear plants to receive a second 20-year license renewal.

name: Susquehanna Steam Electric Station
strategicNote: Key asset for Talen Energy and a major contributor to Pennsylvania's carbon-free electricity.

name: Braidwood Generating Station
strategicNote: Part of the largest nuclear fleet in the U.S. (Constellation's Illinois fleet), providing essential power to the Chicago region.

name: Byron Generating Station
strategicNote: Another key component of Illinois' nuclear capacity; its continued operation was secured by state legislative support in 2021.

name: Hoover Dam
strategicNote: Iconic engineering marvel; provides critical power and water regulation for the Southwestern United States (Arizona, Nevada, California).

name: Alta Wind Energy Center
owner: Terra-Gen Power, NRG Energy, and others
strategicNote: One of the largest onshore wind farms in the world; located in the Tehachapi Pass, a major wind corridor in California.
notes: Also known as the Mojave Wind Farm.

name: West County Energy Center
strategicNote: One of the largest and most efficient natural gas plants in the U.S., serving Southern Florida.

name: Ivanpah Solar Electric Generating System
strategicNote: Major concentrated solar thermal project; high visibility as a flagship large-scale solar project on federal lands.
notes: Uses solar power tower technology.

name: Son La Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: Largest hydroelectric plant in Southeast Asia, critical for northern Vietnam's power supply and flood control.

name: Hoa Binh Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: Second largest hydro plant in Vietnam, vital for grid stability and irrigation in the Red River Delta.

name: Lai Chau Hydroelectric Power Plant
strategicNote: Part of the Da River hydropower cascade, essential for energy security in the remote northwest.

name: Phu My Power Center
strategicNote: The largest gas-fired power complex in Vietnam, supplying roughly 40% of the south's electricity.
notes: Includes Phu My 1, 2.1, 2.2, 3, and 4 units.

name: Duyen Hai Power Center
strategicNote: Critical coal power hub in the Mekong Delta, supporting industrial growth in southern Vietnam.
notes: Includes Duyen Hai 1 (1245MW), Duyen Hai 3 (1245MW), and Duyen Hai 3 Extension (688MW). Total capacity reflected is for the operational hub.

name: Vinh Tan Power Center
strategicNote: One of the largest coal complexes in Vietnam, central to the national energy security strategy despite environmental concerns.
notes: Includes Vinh Tan 1, 2, 4, and 4 Extension.

name: Mong Duong Power Center
strategicNote: Major coal hub in Quang Ninh province, leveraging local coal reserves for northern grid stability.
notes: Includes Mong Duong 1 (1080MW) and Mong Duong 2 (1240MW).

name: Nghi Son Power Center
strategicNote: Supports the Nghi Son Economic Zone, a key industrial hub in North-Central Vietnam.
notes: Includes Nghi Son 1 (600MW) and Nghi Son 2 (1200MW).

name: Ca Mau Power Center
strategicNote: Utilizes offshore gas from the PM3-CAA field; critical for the southernmost region's development.
notes: Consists of Ca Mau 1 and Ca Mau 2 plants (750MW each).

name: Nhon Trach Power Center
strategicNote: Key gas power facility serving the industrial clusters of Dong Nai and Ho Chi Minh City.
notes: Includes Nhon Trach 1 (450MW) and Nhon Trach 2 (750MW). Nhon Trach 3 & 4 are under construction.

name: Trung Nam Thuan Nam Solar Farm
strategicNote: One of the largest solar installations in Southeast Asia; includes a critical 500kV substation for regional integration.

name: Ninh Thuan 1 Nuclear Power Plant
strategicNote: Vietnam's first planned nuclear site. Although suspended in 2016, it remains in long-term strategic plans (PDP8) for potential revival.
notes: Currently suspended but of high geopolitical interest due to former Russian partnership.

name: Koeberg Nuclear Power Station
strategicNote: The only nuclear power plant on the African continent, providing reliable base-load power to the Western Cape.
notes: Located on the Atlantic coast north of Cape Town.

name: Kusile Power Station
strategicNote: One of the world's largest coal-fired power stations, critical for addressing South Africa's energy crisis.
notes: Includes advanced flue-gas desulphurization (FGD) technology.

name: Medupi Power Station
strategicNote: The largest dry-cooled coal-fired power station in the world, essential for power security in South Africa.

name: Kendal Power Station
strategicNote: Strategically important for its massive capacity and unique dry-cooling system.

name: Lethabo Power Station
strategicNote: Notable for its ability to burn low-grade coal, maximizing the use of domestic resources.

name: Majuba Power Station
strategicNote: One of Eskom's newer coal plants, significantly contributing to the national grid.

name: Matimba Power Station
strategicNote: A major coal facility in the Limpopo province, utilizing nearby coal reserves.

name: Tutuka Power Station
strategicNote: Significant coal-fired plant in the Mpumalanga province.

name: Matla Power Station
strategicNote: One of the older workhorses of the Eskom fleet.

name: Duvha Power Station
strategicNote: Major contributor to South Africa's coal-fired generation capacity.

name: Kriel Power Station
strategicNote: Important large-scale coal plant in the Mpumalanga coalfields.

name: Arnot Power Station
strategicNote: One of South Africa's long-serving coal power plants.

name: Drakensberg Pumped Storage Scheme
strategicNote: Critical facility for grid stability and energy storage in South Africa.

name: Ingula Pumped Storage Scheme
strategicNote: A modern pumped-storage scheme vital for managing peak demand.

name: Gariep Dam
strategicNote: South Africa's largest hydroelectric facility.

name: Iron Gates I Hydroelectric Power Station
